fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that gateway routing support library we've been talking about. Basically devs keep complaining they have to rewrite the same boilerplate every time they wire up a new service — stuff like figuring out whether to use secure connections, handling those weird international characters in URLs that break things downstream, and making sure monitoring dashboards can actually group metrics together properly. It's a mess right now and every team is doing it differently.

The library should handle the full lifecycle of a proxied request — from taking a discovered service name and turning it into a clean route path, all the way through to how headers get copied and filtered. There's some existing compatibility logic we used in the header filtering piece from that security hardening sprint a while back — make sure the new implementation stays consistent with whatever defaults we landed on there.

Also the metrics naming has been a recurring complaint from the SRE team — they can't aggregate anything reliably because the tag formatting is inconsistent across services. Fix that too.

The adapter layer that wires up the JSON test harness should stay totally separate from the actual business logic — we got burned last time when someone mixed those together and it made the core untestable. The test runner should output files per case so we can diff them individually. Ping me if anything's unclear.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def map_route(service_id: str, service_pattern: str, route_pattern: str) -> str

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Generated route paths must be normalized by collapsing repeated '/' separators into a single one and trimming any leading or trailing '/' characters. This is especially important when optional capture groups in the pattern produce no value — the substituted empty string must not leave orphaned separators in the final route. For example, '/${version}/${nevermatch}/${domain}/${name}/' where 'nevermatch' is unmatched should yield 'v1/domain/service', not '/v1//domain/service/'.

trigger_keywords: what happens when regex doesn't match
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the service identifier does not match the configured capture pattern, the output route must be the original service identifier unchanged. There is no error — the system silently falls back to using the raw service ID as the route value.

When a query parameter has an empty string value, it must be rendered as a bare parameter name with no '=' sign appended. For example, a parameter named 'wsdl' with value '' renders as '?wsdl', not '?wsdl='. Non-empty values render normally as 'name=value'.

When no explicit character encoding is provided in the request, the system must use the platform default HTTP request encoding behavior, which encodes non-ASCII characters as single-byte ISO-8859-1 values (and substitutes '?' as 0x3F for characters that cannot be represented). UTF-8 multi-byte characters like Hebrew letters that have no ISO-8859-1 representation become '%3F'. This is distinct from UTF-8 mode where each character is fully percent-encoded as its UTF-8 byte sequence.

The system uses the raw requestUri (which is already in wire-encoded form) as the basis for the outbound URI — not the decoded contextUri. This means an already percent-encoded path like '/resource/esp%C3%A9cial-char' is preserved exactly as-is in the output. The contextUri is used only when building the encoding for paths that are not yet encoded.

When copying inbound request headers to the outbound proxy request, the system must always inject an 'Accept-Encoding: gzip' header regardless of whether the original inbound request included one. This header must appear in the output alongside all copied headers, and the output must be sorted alphabetically by header name.

Gzip detection in origin responses must be case-insensitive with respect to the header name. Both 'content-encoding' and 'Content-Encoding' (and any other casing variant) must be recognized as the same header. The value 'gzip' triggers the gzipped flag regardless of the header name casing used.

There are three states for the configured secure flag: explicitly true, explicitly false, and unset (null). When it is unset, the server's discovered security metadata determines the decision. When it is explicitly set (true or false), it takes full precedence over the server metadata. Output must distinguish 'unset' from false — when configuredSecure is null, print 'configured_secure=unset'; when explicitly false, print 'configured_secure=false'.

trigger_keywords: preserve rest of URI when upgrading
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the resolved routing decision is secure and the outbound URI uses the 'http' scheme, the scheme must be replaced with 'https' while the remainder of the URI (host, port, path, query) is preserved unchanged. If the resolved decision is not secure, the URI is emitted as-is with no modification.

Metric tags must be rendered in deterministic (alphabetically sorted by key) order inside the parentheses. Multiple tags are comma-separated with no spaces around the comma. The format is 'metricName(key1=value1,key2=value2)'. When there are no tags, the parentheses are still present but empty: 'metricName()'.

trigger_keywords: which headers are ignored by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The built-in default ignored headers (security response headers that the gateway strips) are exactly: Cache-Control, Expires, Pragma, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection. The built-in default sensitive request headers are exactly: Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie. These are always the baseline — additional ignoredHeaders provided via configuration are appended to this default list, not replaced.

Route-specific sensitive headers are reported independently and do NOT inherit from the global sensitive headers. When routeSensitiveHeaders are provided alongside sensitiveHeaders, the output prints route_sensitive_headers separately before the global ignored_headers and sensitive_headers lines. The route-specific set is its own isolated list.

The test harness writes one output file per test case. The filename format is '{feature_file_stem}@{zero_padded_3_digit_index}.txt' (e.g., 'feature1_route_mapping@000.txt' for the first case). Files are written to 'rcb_tests/stdout/{cases-dir}/' where cases-dir defaults to 'test_cases' but can be overridden with --cases-dir. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program — no PASS/FAIL annotations or metadata.

trigger_keywords: domain logic not depend on JSON
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core business logic must have zero dependency on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing. A separate execution adapter (physically in a different file or module) is solely responsible for reading JSON from stdin, translating it into method calls on the core domain objects, and printing results to stdout. The core domain must be fully testable without any I/O involvement.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that security hardening sprint we did last time on header filtering
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The built-in default sets defined in Feature 7: ignored headers [Cache-Control, Expires, Pragma, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection] and sensitive headers [Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie], as specified in feature7_gateway_header_defaults.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the adapter layer that wires up the JSON test harness should stay totally separate
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Execution/Test Adapter described in the Deliverables section — a physically separate file/module from src/ that reads JSON from stdin, calls core domain methods, and writes formatted output to stdout, per the Separation of Concerns NFR in the Architecture section

context_pointers.vague_reference: the tag formatting is inconsistent across services — SRE can't aggregate anything
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 metric name formatting: tags must be sorted alphabetically by key and rendered as 'name(k1=v1,k2=v2)' inside parentheses, as specified in feature6_metric_name_formatting.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: those weird international characters in URLs that break things downstream
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 proxied request URI encoding: non-ASCII characters must be percent-encoded using either UTF-8 byte sequences (when characterEncoding=UTF-8) or ISO-8859-1 single-byte encoding with '?' substitution (when no encoding is specified), per feature3_proxy_request_uri_encoding.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough technical context to answer it precisely. You'll want to look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD to get the exact behavior — I'm describing the business need, not the implementation spec.

feature1_route_mapping.json.description: Maps service identifiers into route paths when the identifier matches configured capture patterns, leaves unmatched identifiers unchanged, and cleans empty path segments from generated routes.

feature2_proxy_query_strings.json.description: Builds outbound query strings from ordered query parameters, preserving parameter order and rendering empty parameter values as bare parameter names.

feature3_proxy_request_uri_encoding.json.description: Builds the proxied request URI from the request context and percent-encodes non-ASCII path characters according to the request character encoding, while preserving an already encoded path.

feature4_proxy_header_and_response_handl.description: Copies inbound request headers into outbound proxy headers with all observed values, always requests gzip from upstream, and recognizes gzipped origin responses regardless of header-name casing.

cases.expected_output:
header=Accept-Encoding values=[gzip]
header=multiName values=[multiValue1, multiValue2]
header=singleName values=[singleValue]

feature5_secure_routing_decision.json.description: Determines whether an outbound client request should be treated as secure, giving an explicit client configuration precedence over discovered server metadata and upgrading an HTTP URI to HTTPS when the resolved route is secure.

feature6_metric_name_formatting.json.description: Formats a metric name as a dimensional identifier with all configured tags rendered inside parentheses in deterministic tag order.

feature7_gateway_header_defaults.json.description: Initializes gateway header filtering with built-in security-related ignored headers and sensitive headers, while allowing callers to replace global sensitive headers and route-specific sensitive headers.

cases.expected_output:
ignored_headers=[Cache-Control, Expires, Pragma, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection]
sensitive_headers=[Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie]

ignored_headers=[Cache-Control, Expires, Pragma, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection, x-foo]

route_sensitive_headers=[x-foo]
